findings.description: Direct architectural contradiction: Overview states 'All pipeline stages run in parallel to maximize throughput' while Stage 2 states 'Stage 2 must complete before Stage 3 begins.' The pipeline cannot be both fully parallel and have a sequential Stage 2→3 dependency.
findings.evidence: Overview: 'All pipeline stages run in parallel to maximize throughput.' Stage 2: 'Stage 2 must complete before Stage 3 begins.'
findings.suggestion: Determine the actual architecture. If stages 1-2 and 3-4 are parallel within their groups but 2→3 is sequential, state that explicitly. Update both locations to agree.

findings.description: Dangling reference: 'Security considerations are documented in Section 6.' The document has no Section 6 — sections are Overview, Architecture Components, Performance Characteristics, Operational Notes, Conclusions.
findings.evidence: 'Security considerations are documented in Section 6.' (Stage 4). No section numbered or titled Section 6 exists anywhere in the document.
findings.suggestion: Add a Security section as Section 6, or remove the dangling reference and add security content inline or in a new section.

findings.description: Security architecture is absent from a customer-data pipeline processing events at scale. Authentication, authorization, encryption in transit and at rest, and data isolation are required components of a final architecture document for a system processing customer data.
findings.evidence: No security architecture section exists. The only security reference is 'Authentication is used to verify each request' (Stage 1) and the broken Section 6 reference (Stage 4). No encryption, authorization, or data isolation policy is described.
findings.suggestion: Add a Security section covering: authentication mechanism, authorization model, encryption (in transit and at rest), data isolation, and access controls.

findings.description: 'Transformation applies the standard retry policy on transient failures.' The retry policy is referenced by name but never defined. Max attempts, backoff type, backoff intervals, and applicable error conditions are not specified anywhere in the document.
findings.evidence: 'Transformation applies the standard retry policy on transient failures.' — no definition of 'standard retry policy' in any section of the document.
findings.suggestion: Define the retry policy inline or in a dedicated configuration section: max attempts, backoff type (linear/exponential), backoff intervals, and which error conditions trigger retry.

findings.description: 'The in-memory delivery cache reduces end-to-end latency by 99.9% compared to unbuffered writes.' A 99.9% latency reduction is an extraordinary claim with no supporting benchmark, methodology, or measurement conditions. No evidence provided.
findings.evidence: 'reduces end-to-end latency by 99.9% compared to unbuffered writes' — no benchmark data, no measurement conditions, no source cited.
findings.suggestion: Either provide supporting benchmark data with measurement conditions, qualify the claim as approximate or order-of-magnitude, or remove the specific percentage if unverifiable.

findings.description: 'Benchmarks show that batch processing is 3× faster than streaming for files under 10MB. For larger payloads, streaming is preferred.' The reasoning reversal is unexplained. If batch is faster for small files, what property of larger files makes streaming preferable? The logic is incomplete.
findings.evidence: 'batch processing is 3× faster than streaming for files under 10MB. For larger payloads, streaming is preferred.' — no explanation of why the preference reverses at the 10MB threshold.
findings.suggestion: Explain the reversal (e.g., memory constraints, parallelism characteristics, queue throughput limits for large payloads) to make the reasoning complete and defensible.

findings.description: 'Large files are handled separately from the primary transformation path via an overflow queue.' Two gaps: (1) 'Large files' threshold is undefined — what size triggers the overflow path? (2) The overflow queue's behavior is unspecified: destination, delivery guarantees, capacity, and expected latency are all absent.
findings.evidence: 'Large files are handled separately from the primary transformation path via an overflow queue.' — no size threshold, no downstream specification for overflow queue.
findings.suggestion: Define the size threshold triggering overflow, and specify overflow queue behavior: destination stage, delivery guarantee, capacity, and expected latency relative to primary path.

findings.description: 'malformed events are rejected with a 400 response and logged to the dead-letter queue.' Dead-letter queue handling is entirely unspecified: no alerting, no retry eligibility, no inspection process, no purge policy. In production ETL systems, DLQ handling is an operational requirement.
findings.evidence: 'malformed events are rejected with a 400 response and logged to the dead-letter queue.' — no further mention of DLQ handling anywhere in the document.
findings.suggestion: Add DLQ handling specification: alerting thresholds, inspection/review process, retry eligibility criteria, and purge/retention policy.

findings.description: Performance claims 'sub-second end-to-end latency for 95th-percentile events' while Stage 3 describes '5-minute polling interval' for batch completion. These are compatible but the relationship is unexplained — a reader would reasonably wonder how sub-second event latency coexists with 5-minute batch polling.
findings.evidence: 'sub-second end-to-end latency for 95th-percentile events' (Performance) vs. 'Polling for batch completion uses a 5-minute interval' (Stage 3).
findings.suggestion: Clarify that end-to-end latency refers to event processing latency (ingestion to transformation), while batch completion polling is a delivery confirmation mechanism. These are different metrics.

findings.description: 'Authentication is used to verify each request before processing begins.' Authentication mechanism is not specified. For a system processing external customer data, the authentication type (API key, OAuth 2.0, mTLS, etc.) is a required architectural decision.
findings.evidence: 'Authentication is used to verify each request before processing begins.' — no mechanism, token type, or protocol specified.
findings.suggestion: Specify the authentication mechanism and protocol.

findings.description: 'Errors are logged and the batch is requeued for up to 3 retry attempts.' Post-exhaustion behavior is not specified. After 3 failed retries, what happens: data loss, dead-letter queue, operator alert, indefinite hold?
findings.evidence: 'the batch is requeued for up to 3 retry attempts.' — no specification of what happens when all 3 are exhausted.
findings.suggestion: Specify post-retry-exhaustion behavior: DLQ routing, operator alerting, data loss policy, or manual intervention process.

findings.description: Document claims 50,000 events per second peak load but provides no capacity sizing for components that must sustain this throughput: Redis cache throughput, message queue depth, data warehouse write throughput, transformation worker count.
findings.evidence: 'handles approximately 50,000 events per second under peak load' (Overview) — no corresponding capacity specifications in any component description.
findings.suggestion: Add capacity requirements or sizing guidance per component relative to peak load. At minimum, note which components are the bottleneck and how scaling addresses them.

findings.description: Delivery semantics are not stated. At-least-once, at-most-once, or exactly-once? For an analytics ETL pipeline, delivery semantics are a fundamental architectural property determining correctness of downstream analytics. The deduplication window implies at-least-once with bounded dedup, but this is not stated.
findings.evidence: No statement of delivery guarantees anywhere in the document. Deduplication (Stage 2) and retry (Stage 3) exist but their combined semantic is not declared.
findings.suggestion: State the delivery semantic explicitly (e.g., 'The pipeline provides at-least-once delivery with deduplication within a 5-minute window, yielding effectively-once semantics for events within the window') and describe how the architecture achieves it.

findings.description: No formal component inventory exists. Key infrastructure components (Redis cache, message queue, dead-letter queue, overflow queue, reference database, data warehouse) are introduced inline within stage descriptions but never listed as named components with their roles.
findings.evidence: Redis cache appears in Stage 2; message queue in Stage 1; dead-letter queue in Stage 1; overflow queue in Stage 2; reference database in Stage 2; data warehouse in Stage 3 — none appear in a components list.
findings.suggestion: Add a component inventory or dependency table listing each infrastructure component, its role, and which stages it serves.

findings.description: Document claims 'fault-tolerant data delivery path' but provides no measurable availability target, error budget, or SLA. For a final architecture document, these are expected.
findings.evidence: 'fault-tolerant data delivery path' (Conclusions) — no SLA, uptime target, or error budget defined anywhere.
findings.suggestion: Add an availability section or SLA statement: target uptime, error budget, RTO/RPO if applicable.

findings.description: 'deduplicates events within a 5-minute window' — the 5-minute deduplication window is a specific technical parameter with no rationale or configuration reference provided.
findings.evidence: '5-minute window' (Stage 2) — no justification or configuration section.
findings.suggestion: Add rationale for the 5-minute window or reference the configuration parameter that controls it.

findings.description: 'Future work includes adding a real-time streaming workflow alongside the existing batch pipeline.' Roadmap content in the conclusions of a final architecture document is out of scope.
findings.evidence: 'Future work includes adding a real-time streaming workflow alongside the existing batch pipeline.' (Conclusions paragraph).
findings.suggestion: Move to a Future State section or remove from this document.

findings.description: 'Security considerations are documented in Section 6.' — security reference is out of register for a monitoring section and also a dangling reference (see F-002). The sentence breaks the reading flow of the monitoring description.
findings.evidence: Stage 4 describes metrics endpoints and polling intervals, then inserts 'Security considerations are documented in Section 6.' which belongs in a security section, not monitoring.
findings.suggestion: Remove this sentence from Stage 4; add actual security content in a dedicated section.

evaluate_decision: CONTINUE — score 5 < threshold 95, iterations remaining: 3. Proceed to FIX.
